titleStmt.title: Letter from Robert Carson to Ivor Guest, 22 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Robert Carson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Robert Carson to Lord Wimborne (Ivor Guest) (1873-1938). Mr. Carson is concerned about the development of the resources and wealth of the country but he has found the Irish people hopeless to deal with. He is offering his services to the Crown to provide ‘proper and honest management in development of the resources’, thereby providing honest employment for the people to the benefit of the Country. A comment, added by the CSO, indicates that this letter ‘calls for no notice’.Included in the upload are two enclosures :1. A newspaper cutting showing a letter (to the Editor) written by Mr. Carson outlining his proposal to harness Lough Neagh and the consequent savings.2. A Preliminary Circular which would form the basis of a Memorandum and Articles of Association for a company called Industrial Development and Trading Company Limited, set up by Mr. Carson, with broad-ranging objectives of industrial development. Ivor Churchill Guest, 1st Viscount Wimborne (1873-1939) was appointed the Lord Lieutenant of Ireland in 1915. Guest worked closely alongside Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) in the administration of Ireland. Following the suppression of the Rising, Guest, under pressure, was forced to resign as Lord Lieutenant. A later inquiry into the Rising exonerated Guest and held Augustine Birrell (1850-1933) chiefly responsible for the events that occurred. Guest would continue to serve as Lord Lieutenant until 1918.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/7288

correspAction.placeName: 7 Waring Street, Belfast, County Down, Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7 Waring Street Belfast April 22 nd 1916 Lord Wimborne Vice Regal Lodge, Dublin My Lord I see from the newspapers you want to arrange for
the extension of industries in Ireland after the war. I have spent years trying to get the Irish people to
develop the resources and the wealth of the country but
I have found them hopeless to deal with. Ireland under proper and honest management in
developing the resources of the country can provide fully
Â£20,000,000 a year for next 100 years without increasing
the taxation on the people. I know Ireland well for many years and where the
material is for making the sum named and I offer my
services to the crown. I can take charge of the undertaking
and get it started on business lines and provide some
honest employment for the people to the benefit of the country. Waiting for your reply, I am my Lord.
yours faithfully Robert Carson Asst. Under Secretary
This letter calls I think for no notice
WML 4/5
HARNESSING LOUGH NEAGH.
(To the Editor.) Belfast has long been looked upon as the
most goâahead and enterprising city in Ireâ
land. But why not take advantage of the
power that Nature has provided, and which
has been running waste for generations, undâ
eveloped, and which, when harnessed, would
provide a revenue for the city of over
Â£1,00,000 a year. The tramway loss, and the
sliding gardens muddle are a small item comâ
pared with the stupendous waste going on day
by day. Count it up for the past 100 years â
Â£100,000,000 lost to Belfast. There are no engineering
difficulties in the way. Lough
Neagh, the largest lake in the United Kingâ
dom, is 18 miles distant from Belfast, is fully
50 feet above sea level at low water mark, and
has an area of 340 square miles. Taking the
high water level of the lake at 54 feet, the
area would be about 500 square miles. Ten
rivers flow into it, and there is only one outâ âlet âthe Lower Bann. This vast water power
has been passing to the sea for thousands of
years as waste. It has been estimated that
Belfast could have 100,000 horse power six
months of the year, 50,000 three months, and
30,000 h.p. the other three months (a dry sumâ
mer). Take the present cost of horse power
at Â£15 per h.p. per year. This would produce
Â£1,050,000 a year. Set aside Â£100,000 for inâ
terest on capital and cost of plant, and
Â£100,000 for sinking fund, that would leave a
balance of Â£850,000 a year to credit of the
city income. After twenty years the entire
cost of plant and undertaking would be paid
off by sinking fund, when the entire income of
over Â£1,000,000 a year would go to credit
of city revenue. The vast water power would
be conveyed part of the way by open
canal, and part of the way by pipes
from Lough Neagh to the power station.
situate on the Lagan, where turbines,
dynamos, and storage batteries would be erecâ
ted and electricity generated to be distributed
to tramways, shipyards, mills, factories, and
for town lighting. Manufacturers and millâ
owners who now provide their own motive
power by steam and pay a high price for coal
and upkeep of their steam plant would unâ
doubtedly use electric power, for by so doing
they would get three times the power for the
same cost. The smoke nuisance would be
abated and the health conditions of the city
improved. The water, after leaving the power
station, would scour out the Lagan, the harâ
bour, and the straight channel to the deep
sea, carrying all sewerage with it, and make
the constant dredging operations now carried
on by the harbour authorities unnecessary.
This undertaking would also relieve the large
area of lands round Lough Neagh shores and
the lowâlying lands along the Lower Bann
from periodical flooding. Many thousands of
acres now of little value would become valuable,
as the water-level of Lough Neagh could
be kept at a certain height winter and summer.
Large sums of money have been spent from
time to time by the Board of Works cutting
canals here and there, with the result that
much land became flooded which had been free
from flooding formerly. An Act of Parliaâ
ment will be required, but it is worth all the
expense. It is now up to the City Council,
Harbour Commissioners, propertyâowners, and
the ratepayers of the city to secure this great
income without delay, and make Belfast a
city free of rates and taxes. â Yours, &c., ROBERT CARSON. 7 Waring Street, Belfast. Sept 10 â 1915
Evg Tele
1907 ROBERT CARSON, C.E. 7 WARING STREET, BELFAST. Preliminary Circular
INDUSTRIAL DEVELOPMENT AND TRADING COMPANY, LIMITED
(Incorporated under the Joint Stock Companies Acts, 1862 to 1908). CAPITAL â Â£500,000.
DIVIDED INTO
100,000 Ordinary Shares of 1/â each
250,000 Guaranteed and fully secured 5% 'A' Cumulative
Preference SHares of Â£1 each.
245,000 'B' 6% Cumulative Preerence Shares of Â£1 each. The 'A' Preference Shares will be fully secured as to Capital and dividends, and
will rank in priority to the 'B' Preference Shares. PRESENT ISSUE OF
250,000 Guaranteed and fully secured 5 p.c. 'A' Cumulative Preference Shares of Â£1 each.
245,000 6 per cet. Cumulative Preference Shares of Â£1 each PAYABLE AS FOLLOWS : â
2/6 per Share on application.
2/6 per Share on allotment.
5/â per Share Two Months after allotment.
And the Balance in calls not exceeding 5/- per Share at intervals of not less than two months. Subscribers may pay up in full on allotment.
The Ordinary Shares will be taken up by Mr. Carson and his friends. The objects for which the Company is formed are :â To revive old and start new industries
in districts where experience considers there is a reasonable prospect of success. To develop the
resources in the country, to take up Estates, waste lands, mountains, bogs, woods, waterpowers,
rivers, lakes, minerals, mining, clay earths, quarries, lands, etc.; to take up and develop all classes
of manufactures, to promote companies for like purposes, to finance or assist in working or developâ
ing same ; to underwrite, guarantee contracts and undertakings, to take up Patents, work and
develop same ; or promote companies to do so ; to buy or sell Patents either for cah or shares ; to
manufacture all classes of material. Engineers, ship, boat, barge owners and builders. Hotels,
recreation and sports of various kinds. Stock and share delers, brokers and agents. Railways,
tramways ; traction engones and trains. Electric, steam, oil, gas, power and lighting, etc. To act
as carriers, warehousemen ; to discount bills, to borrow and lend money, to erect or pull down
buildings, to make contacts for all classes of work and carry through or sublet the same or any
portion thereof ; to make canals, work, sublet or sell them, acquire by purchase or otherwise ;
reclaim lands, labour, sublet, work or sell the same for any purpose and on any terms. Merchants,
manufacturers, traders in all kinds of produce and material. Importers, exporters of all or any class
or kinds of materials and other articles of commerce. To provide employment for many thousands
of the people of the country, and help put a stop to emigration by providing plenty of employâ
ment for the people at home. To encourage agriculture in every way, providing markets for the
purchase of farmers' produce, to buy and sell the same ; to lend money to farmers and others,
to lend money on securities or otherwise, etc. The foregoing are a few of the powers I take in the Memorandum and Artices of Association.
Your assistance and support is respectfully invited. The investment is one of the soundest and
safest ever put on the market. Real Estate assets to double the amount of entire subscribed
capital will be vested in 'Trustees', with full power and control to safeguard the A and B Preference
Shareholders' interests. It is expected that these shares will go up in prce to 40/â within three
months after allotment. Some o the industries to be stated will make a very large profit, fully
25% on their capitals required to work the different lines of businesses. Large orders are already in
hand waiting the starting of 'Plants' and machinery. I will be able to supply any further information to gentlemen who wish to take an active
part in this undertaking, either as Directors or Subscribers. A large amount of capital is already
promised ; the Board of Directors will shortly be seected. Gentlemen who wish to join the Board
will kindly send in their names to the undersigned, and much oblige, Yours faithfully, ROBERT CARSON
